remarks.title: Strengthening Africa’s Geopolitical Agency and Trade Resilience in a Multipolar World
remarks.session: Opening and Welcoming Remarks; Global Chief Economists Network Annual Meeting and Inaugural African Chief Economists Network Meeting

summary.executive_summary: The speaker welcomed participants to the 2026 African Economic Conference, the annual meeting of the Global Chief Economists Network, and the inaugural meeting of the African Chief Economists Network. He framed the gathering as taking place during a fundamental restructuring of the global order, marked by shifting trade patterns, reorganized supply chains, accelerating technological competition, fragmented financial flows, and rising geopolitical tensions. In this environment, Africa must move beyond narratives of dependency, vulnerability, and adaptation toward agency, resilience, competitiveness, and influence. The speaker argued that Africa’s geopolitical agency will depend on its ability to negotiate from a position of strength, shape international rules, convert collective knowledge into coordinated action, and influence global outcomes. Priorities include mobilizing capital at scale, strengthening Africa’s financial architecture, amplifying the continent’s voice in global economic governance, developing human capital, building resilient infrastructure and logistics, expanding regional value chains, and creating higher-value industries. He also highlighted the launch of the African Chief Economists Network, certification initiatives for public-finance and macroeconomic-policy officials, and a proposed African financial architecture for development. He urged participants to challenge conventional thinking, build partnerships, and generate practical, evidence-based solutions for an Africa that develops on its own terms.
summary.purpose: To welcome participants, frame the strategic importance of the conference, introduce major institutional initiatives, and call for coordinated, practical action to strengthen Africa’s agency and resilience.

main_themes.description: Africa should move from reacting to global developments toward shaping rules, negotiations, and outcomes.
Trade, supply chains, technology, finance, and geopolitics are being reorganized, creating both risks and opportunities for Africa.
Africa needs stronger coordination and financial institutions capable of mobilizing, leveraging, and deploying capital at scale.
Chief economists, policy institutions, universities, and public officials should harmonize diagnostics and strengthen evidence-based policy advice.
Africa must invest in infrastructure, logistics, regional value chains, innovation, entrepreneurship, and higher-value industries.

key_messages.message: Africa must shift from dependency and adaptation toward agency, resilience, competitiveness, and influence.
Africa’s agency will be measured by its ability to negotiate from strength, shape rules, coordinate action, and influence outcomes.
A stronger African financial architecture is essential to mobilize capital and amplify Africa’s voice in global economic governance.
Human capital must be transformed into productivity, innovation, entrepreneurship, and leadership.
Resilient infrastructure, stronger logistics, regional value chains, and higher-value industries are central to structural transformation.
Participants should challenge conventional thinking and focus on practical, evidence-based solutions and partnerships.

priorities_and_challenges: Responding to fragmented trade, finance, supply chains, and geopolitical tensions.
Strengthening Africa’s bargaining power and influence in global economic governance.
Mobilizing capital at the scale required for structural transformation.
Improving infrastructure, logistics, skills, innovation, and regional value chains.
Turning research, data, and collective expertise into coordinated policy action.
commitments_and_announcements: Launch of the African Chief Economists Network in collaboration with partner institutions.
Graduation and certification of 435 officials from more than 36 African countries through public-finance and macroeconomic-policy academies.
Advancement of a new African financial architecture for development to improve coordination and mobilize capital at scale.
Continued collaboration among the African Development Bank Group, UNDP, OECD, UNECA, and other partners.
policy_implications: African institutions should coordinate economic diagnostics and policy advice more closely.
Regional and continental bodies should strengthen Africa’s representation in global economic decision-making.
Governments should prioritize infrastructure, logistics, regional value chains, and value-added industries.
Development finance should be organized to mobilize, leverage, and deploy capital more effectively.
Policy choices should be grounded in African knowledge systems, research, and evidence.
calls_to_action: Explore innovative and practical policy solutions.
Forge new partnerships across African and international institutions.
Use coordinated action to advance inclusive and sustainable development.
partnerships_and_stakeholders: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development
United Nations Economic Commission for Africa
African multilateral and development finance institutions
African economists and former senior policymakers
summary.concluding_message: The speaker called for a structurally transformed and resilient Africa that develops on its own terms, draws on its own knowledge and evidence, and uses smart partnerships to accelerate inclusive and sustainable development.

powerful_quotes.quote: Today, we must focus on agency, resilience, competitiveness, and influence.
powerful_quotes.context: Contrasting a forward-looking vision with older narratives of dependency and vulnerability.

powerful_quotes.quote: Africa has moved from being affected by global developments to helping to shape them.
powerful_quotes.context: Describing the continent’s emerging role in the global economy.

powerful_quotes.quote: I encourage all participants to challenge conventional thinking, explore innovative ideas, forge new partnerships, and focus on practical solutions.
powerful_quotes.context: Calling participants to use the conference for action-oriented collaboration.

powerful_quotes.quote: A structurally transformed and resilient Africa that develops on its own terms.
powerful_quotes.context: Presenting the long-term vision for Africa’s development.

highlights.text: Africa must move from dependency and adaptation toward agency and influence.
The African Chief Economists Network is being launched to improve policy coordination and advisory support.
435 officials from more than 36 countries are to be certified in public-finance and macroeconomic-policy management.
Africa needs a stronger financial architecture to mobilize and deploy capital at scale.
Participants were urged to generate practical solutions and partnerships grounded in African knowledge and evidence.
The concluding vision is an inclusive, sustainable, structurally transformed, and resilient Africa.

uncertain_items: Several names and institutional titles in the opening acknowledgements are poorly transcribed.
The transcript appears to render 'multipolar world' inconsistently as 'multicultural' or 'mid-temporal' world.
The exact formal name and acronym of the proposed African financial architecture are unclear in the transcript.
The names of some partner institutions associated with the African Chief Economists Network are indistinct.
The speaker’s name is not clearly transcribed in the SRT and was normalized from the event context.
